title: One Small Money Conversation with a Child This Week
subtitle: An activity practice for starting the money education you want your children to have
excerpt: A single, low-stakes money conversation with a child this week. Not a lecture, one sentence, one moment, one step toward normalising money as a thing families talk about clearly.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Draws on public-domain developmental psychology research showing that normalised, age-appropriate money talk reduces financial anxiety in children.

content.text: One small money conversation with a child this week. Not a curriculum, not a lecture, one ordinary moment where money is named clearly and without drama. The activity builds the habit before the children are old enough to need the full version of the conversation.

The money education children receive is mostly ambient, the flinch at a price, the silence when a number is named, the way adults leave the room when money talk arrives. One small deliberate conversation counters that ambient education with something chosen. Over a year, those small moments accumulate into a different inheritance.
Pick one ordinary moment this week. At the shops. Paying a bill. Getting paid. Choosing what to buy.

Name what's happening in one clear sentence. "I'm choosing the own-brand because it's the same thing for less money and I'm pleased about that." Or: "I just got paid for the work I did last month and I'm going to put some of it straight into savings."
Don't explain. Don't moralize. Don't ask them to repeat it back. Just let the sentence land as ordinary information.
Notice how it felt to say it without drama. That is the practice.
Written for homemade.education. Built on the idea that small, calm money talk in front of children shapes how they feel about money later.
